title: NSW invests $2 million in Hilltops Vineyard for dealcoholisation facility
summary:
The NSW government has invested $2 million for Freeman Vineyards in Young to establish what it claims is "the state’s first dealcoholisation facility". The facility aims to position the Hilltops region as a national leader in the rapidly growing non-alcoholic wine market. Freeman Vineyards was one of nine recipients of funding through the Agriculture Industries Innovation and Growth Program, with owner and founder Dr Brian Freeman and his daughter Marcelle Freeman saying the project will be a game changer for the industry in NSW. “We are really excited. You can make much better-quality wine if you don’t have to transport it far, so we can make much better-quality non-alcoholic wine if we don’t have to transport it far,” Marcelle said. “We’re also putting in a bottling unit, so that can help local producers as well, if they want. We will definitely need to hire more people locally once the facility is up and running.”

content:
LTR: Dr Brian Freeman, Steph Cooke MP and Marcelle Freeman. Image supplied.

The NSW government has invested $2 million for Freeman Vineyards in Young to establish what it claims is “the state’s first dealcoholisation facility”.

The facility aims to position the Hilltops region as a national leader in the rapidly growing non-alcoholic wine market.

Freeman Vineyards was one of nine recipients of funding through the Agriculture Industries Innovation and Growth Program, with owner and founder Dr Brian Freeman and his daughter Marcelle Freeman saying the project will be a game changer for the industry in NSW.

“We are really excited. You can make much better-quality wine if you don’t have to transport it far, so we can make much better-quality non-alcoholic wine if we don’t have to transport it far,” Marcelle said.

“We’re also putting in a bottling unit, so that can help local producers as well, if they want.

“We will definitely need to hire more people locally once the facility is up and running.”

The dealcoholisation project and bottling unit means producers no longer need to send their wine interstate for alcohol removal – they can have it made, dealcoholised and bottled all in one place.

Dr Freeman said the facility will help meet growing demand as more consumers, particularly younger people, move towards low and no alcohol options.

“It’s said to be the young generation abstaining from alcohol totally, so there’s that aspect coming into it, as well as the older generation are drinking a lot less.

“But it’s the Hilltops region that has a big advantage, and we’re producing really top-quality grapes, so we can produce a very good quality product on our range of wines that we currently have, but we’ll be able to produce a really good non-alcoholic wine as well,” he said.

Member for Cootamundra Steph Cooke MP also welcomed the investment.

“This is a landmark investment for Freeman Vineyard and the Hilltops wine region, cementing our reputation as a centre for agricultural innovation and premium food and wine production.

“Freeman Vineyards has built an outstanding reputation for producing worldclass wines and this funding will allow them to lead the way in one of the fastest growing segments of the global wine industry,” she said.

Cooke added that the funding is a vote of confidence in the region’s wine industry.

“Establishing New South Wales’s first dealcoholisation facility right here in Young means more value will be added locally, more jobs can be created locally, and more opportunities will flow to our grape growers and winemakers.”

With demand for premium low and no-alcohol wines continuing to grow, Cooke believes the project is a “significant investment into the future of the NSW wine industry”.

“Keeping that capability in regional NSW will reduce transport costs, improve efficiency and strengthen our local manufacturing sector.

“Consumer preferences are evolving and our local producers are evolving with them.

“This funding will deliver long-term benefits not only for Young, but for wineries across the state looking to access this technology closer to home,” she said.

title: New Zealand Winegrowers adds five wine “champions” to roll of Fellows
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) is celebrating its annual honours list by announcing five additions to the roll of NZW Fellows. Fabian Yukich, chair of NZW, says the Fellows are a peer-nominated group of “highly respected and influential individuals” whose leadership, innovation, hard work and passion have helped shape the success of the New Zealand wine industry. “Each year, the Fellows honour recognises a select group of people who have made an outstanding contribution to our industry. The 2026 Fellows have each helped strengthen New Zealand wine in their own way, through their commitment to excellence, generosity of knowledge, and service to the wider wine community,” said Yukich. The 2026 New Zealand Winegrowers Fellows are as follows: Kevin Judd, Lynnette Hudson, Rex Sunde, Philip Gregan and Sam Neill.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers adds five wine “champions” to roll of Fellows appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR, Top to bottom: Lynette Hudson, Kevin Judd, Philip Gregan, Sam Neill and Rex Sunde. Images supplied

New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) is celebrating its annual honours list by announcing five additions to the roll of NZW Fellows.

Fabian Yukich, chair of NZW, says the Fellows are a peer-nominated group of “highly respected and influential individuals” whose leadership, innovation, hard work and passion have helped shape the success of the New Zealand wine industry.

“Each year, the Fellows honour recognises a select group of people who have made an outstanding contribution to our industry. The 2026 Fellows have each helped strengthen New Zealand wine in their own way, through their commitment to excellence, generosity of knowledge, and service to the wider wine community,” said Yukich.

The 2026 New Zealand Winegrowers Fellows are as follows:

  • Kevin Judd – for promoting New Zealand wine and photography

Judd is recognised for his contribution to New Zealand wine’s global reputation, from his role as founding winemaker at Cloudy Bay to establishing Greywacke, and for his distinctive photography capturing the vineyards, regions and people of the industry.

  • Lynnette Hudson – for services to wine education and Pinot Noir

Hudson’s winemaking expertise and passion for Pinot Noir has led to a longstanding contribution to wine education and industry leadership. She has generously shared her knowledge through the Southern Pinot Noir Workshop, which she has been on the committee and deeply involved with for over 25 years.

  • Rex Sunde – for services to viticulture and sustainability

Sunde has contributed immensely to New Zealand Winegrowers’ Vineyard Spray Schedule, and his service has included time as a New Zealand Winegrowers board member, a member of the New Zealand Winegrowers research committee and an executive committee member for Organic Winegrowers New Zealand.

  • Philip Gregan – for extensive service to the industry and to NZW

Gregan’s outstanding contribution to the New Zealand wine industry has been through over four decades of leadership and service. His tenure saw him play a pivotal role in international trade relations, along with steering the industry to an export-focused global success story. After 43 years of service to the organisation, including 35 years as CEO across the Wine Institute and New Zealand Winegrowers, Gregan retired at the end of June 2026.

  • Sam Neill – recognised for promoting New Zealand wine.

As an internationally acclaimed actor and tireless advocate for the industry over many years, Sam has generously used his profile, talents and distinctive voice to promote New Zealand wine, bringing authenticity, warmth and global attention to the people, places and stories behind the industry.

“We are incredibly proud of this year’s Fellows and the contributions they have made,” said Yukich.

“They leave a lasting legacy for the next generation of winegrowers, and I look forward to acknowledging and congratulating them at their induction at the end of August.”

The 2026 Fellows will be inducted at the New Zealand Wine 2026 Celebration Dinner, being held in Pōneke Wellington at the end of August.

content:
Winner Paolo Saccone MS presenting at his exam. Images credit Isabella Wild

Sommeliers Australia has announced Paolo Saccone MS (LUCAS Restaurants, NSW) as the official winner of the ‘2026 Sommelier Challenge: Finding Australia’s Best Sommelier of the Year’.

The title was unveiled Monday night at a sold-out black-tie dinner ceremony hosted at the Lee Ho Fook in Sydney following an “intensive day of national finals”.

“I’m excited and honoured to be named Australia’s Best Sommelier at this year’s Sommelier Challenge. The calibre of this year’s group was incredibly high – there was no room for error in the competition, and it challenged us to be the best we could possibly be. I’m grateful to be included in such a talented group of candidates,” Saccone said.

As the newly crowned winner, Saccone will represent Australia on the global stage, travelling travel to Lisbon in Portugal from 11–17 October 2026 to compete on behalf of Sommeliers Australia at the ‘Association de la Sommellerie Internationale (ASI) Best Sommelier of the World 2026’ competition.

“I’m ready for the task ahead in Portugal. I have a couple of months to train my tasting skills, theory and practical elements to prepare myself for any scenario. It would be a dream come true to come out on top at the Best Sommelier of the World competition and shine a light on the quality of the sommelier industry in Australia,” he said.

Sommeliers Australia said Saccone triumphed over an “exceptionally competitive field of the nation’s top wine professionals”, which included fellow finalists Isaac Pockney (Bennelong, NSW); Clotilde Candusso (Freyja, VIC); Justin Biskup (CARPE VINO, VIC); and wild card finalist Charlie Richards (Agnes, QLD).

The final stage of the competition took place early Monday in the Sydney Tower at the Infinity by Mark Best restaurant. The five finalists faced a “gruelling gauntlet” of written, tasting, practical and theory examinations “designed to test technical knowledge and industry expertise at the absolute highest standards – reflecting Master Sommelier, WSET Diploma, and ASI Diploma levels”.

“The calibre of talent displayed at this year’s Sommelier Challenge has been nothing short of world-class, and we are incredibly proud to crown Saccone as Australia’s Best Sommelier for 2026,” said Sommeliers Australia president Louella Mathews.

“This 2026 Sommelier Challenge is the modern reboot of Australia’s Best Sommelier of the Year. Beyond being a competition, it provides a pathway for professionals to demonstrate their knowledge on the world stage and engage with the global sommelier community. It strengthens Australia’s wine and hospitality sector by elevating standards of service, deepening collective knowledge and fostering the exchange of expertise across peers and the next generation of sommeliers.

“Saccone demonstrated extraordinary technical precision, theoretical depth, and flawless hospitality under immense pressure, and we are confident he will represent our nation with immense pride and distinction in Lisbon this October.”

Mathews also extended her gratitude to the competition’s partners.

“The Sommelier Challenge would not be possible without the generous support of our Gold Partners: Ruinart, House of Arras, Jim Barry and Pewsey Vale.

“Their commitment to excellence in wine service and education helps elevate the profession across Australia. This collaboration reflects a shared dedication to strengthening industry standards, enhancing brand engagement, and fostering meaningful connections with key industry decision-makers throughout the wine and hospitality sectors.”

title: Wine Growth Summit facilitates sharing of international expertise with Aussie industry professionals
summary:
The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia. Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years. Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand. Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries. Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, shared lessons from the Poco Vino brand story. Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them and chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

The post Wine Growth Summit facilitates sharing of international expertise with Aussie industry professionals appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia.

Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years.

Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia

“This increase won’t offset the larger decreases in the bigger markets on a global scale, but for individual businesses willing to diversify, these could be markets worth taking a closer look at,” she said.

Crabb also drew attention to Japan being “second only in white wine consumption to Australia in the Asia Pacific” before detailing other key export countries in the region.

“Looking at Australian exports of white wines to key Asian destinations, you can see that there’s a generally positive trend to destinations like Hong Kong, Thailand, Singapore and South Korea, but what really stands out are the exports to China – they are significantly different now than before the tariffs. 17% of the volume of exports to China is now white wine, and it was 4% in 2020.”

Crabb reassured the audience that it’s not all sour grapes for red wine exports.

“If you exclude China and Hong Kong, Cab Sav exports are up by 30% in value in the last 12 months. Cabernet Sauvignon is growing very well into Canada at the moment, but the next four markets that are growing are Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Indonesia. Basically, the Southeast Asian markets are drinking more premium Cabernet Sauvignon from Australia.

“Red is a key wine style in these markets, and it’s a very similar story to Shiraz. For Shiraz, excluding China and Hong Kong, the growth is 7% year on year. The fastest growing market is Thailand, followed by Singapore, Canada and Malaysia,” she shared.

Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand.

Brendan Carter, Unico Zello winemaker and director of Bottle Shock

“The thing that got me was the culture between different venues…the operators help each other; it’s a part of their culture. The attitude is very much ‘if someone gets excited about wine, they’re going to go into my neighbour’s venue, and that is a net good for everyone’. They even host each other’s events to try to act as activations, so they’re very, very close,” he revealed.

Carter also detailed the lack of a gender equality issue in Thailand’s wine industry.

“I went to about 25 wine bars in a week, across all of them, all of them, the importers, the buyers, owners, sommeliers and the people with actual purchasing power, there was simply no gender problem to speak of. Women aren’t really fighting for a seat at the Thai wine table, because they literally built the table.

“Interestingly, when I questioned this, they thought it was the dumbest question I could ask. It’s not framed as an achievement in Thailand, because it’s just not an issue, which is his own kind of remark.”

As Carter frankly put it, Thailand is “just f*cking around and finding out”.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7

“The best wineries in the world, they capture data. The best wineries capture 92% of email addresses at the cellar door…when we look at the average, it’s about 50% (46%).

“Our top 10% of wineries are emailing their entire customer base an average of 10 times a year. The average winery only sends two and a half emails a year. If you’re going to build lifetime customers, if you’re going to attract people, you need to capture an email, and you need to send it,” he said.

Kamphuis also emphasised the importance of personalising the experience online for customers, suggesting winery websites should recommend products on the home page based on previous purchases and information regarding flavour preferences collected at the cellar door. Including a simple ‘welcome back’ message with the customer’s name when they log in can also be a nice touch.

There were many domestic success stories told, with Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, sharing lessons from the Poco Vino brand story.

Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage

Harper said that the first thing the winery did before planning a new product at all was research. The company spoke to over 3000 people globally to find out what challenges people were facing with wine, what the barriers are, tensions and what the brand is “solving for”.

She stressed the importance of looking outside of wine for inspiration, with the packing and retail display designs for Poco Vino being reportedly influenced by the beauty and cosmetic category, coffee brands and the trend of mini chocolates.

The team behind the little wine also thought about the moments where customers would typically reach for RTDs, spirits and seltzers, asking themselves how wine could play a role in those spaces instead.

“We’ve been in market for eight months, we are now ranged in 17 countries and we’re selling something in the region of 500 bottles every hour of this product – it’s genuinely blown our minds,” Harper shared.

“…but the story of this brand is not the vineyard and the terroir and the provenance – it’s actually just how it fits in your lifestyle and that it’s easy, it’s delicious and it’s accessible…even the tasting notes literally just have these three words on the back, there’s no spiel, we stripped everything back and pulled it back to its kind of most raw but fun state.

“We’re not selling a Shiraz…we’re selling the moment that you’re drinking it. We’re selling joy, we’re selling connection, we’re selling that moment where you’re sitting in that meal…that’s what we’re selling. So, bring that to people, and bring that to people in a really engaging way,” she advised.

Bringing an Adelaide local lens and down-to-Earth spirit to the event, Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them.

Louis Schofield, founder of World Apart Wines

“Today is about growth, and there’s this great paradox of the industry is not growing, but we keep talking about how we grow. For me, growing is not necessarily about making more money, making more wine, you know, getting more employees, whatever it is…but we do still want to diversify and have some different markets.

“We’ve recently got rid of all of our distributors in Australia, and we now sell the wine ourselves in every market, so I’m doing a lot of domestic travel. I think there’s a great question that keeps coming back to me – why do we need to keep growing?

“These are the things that are important to us in our winery. It isn’t trying to get to 10,000 cases, it isn’t trying to make a million dollars a year turnover – it’s being in the winery with our little kids, it’s being resilient, it’s being sustainable and it’s spending time in my garden. That’s what we love. So, that’s what the growth means to us,” he affirmed.

On the topic of expanding into new markets, chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

Alexia Roberts, chief operating officer at Randall Wine Group

“You know, I think COVID taught us all that we could do stuff by teams, and we all thought, ‘beauty, days of getting on planes are over’, but the reality is that nothing is better than having a conversation in person, face-to-face. Showing up for success is absolutely critical.

“This is an incredibly complex market – some people find it too hard, to be honest, because there are so many nuances in this market – but I promise you, if you take the time and effort to understand it by province, not by country, you will be rewarded,” she said.

Roberts also shared how being flexible can lead to unexpected success, using the launch of the One Pound Per Acre brand as an example.

“Our strategy was about growing premium brands globally, and all of a sudden, we were looking at a $9.95 proposition on the shelf for Chardonnay and Shiraz – but guess what, China was closed, and it seemed like a good opportunity at the time. So, we pivoted. There was an opportunity in front of us, and we pivoted, and we built this brand.

“We launched it in September 2023 with two wines, Chardonnay and Shiraz, and we launched it to fill a gap in the Australian category in Ontario. So [in] one province we launched this wine. 18 months from launch date we were the sixth top Australian wine brand by sales in the LCDO.”

title: Yangarra Estate appoints new winemaking and viticulture leadership team
summary:
McLaren Vale’s Yangarra Estate has today announced its new winemaking and viticulture leadership team, appointing Guillaume Camougrand as head of winemaking and viticulture, Shelley Torresan as senior winemaker and Cliff Wickman as vineyard manager. The appointments follow the passing of Peter Fraser, who served as Yangarra's head winemaker and general manager from 2000 to November 2025. Chairman and proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, Barbara Banke, said that Yangarra has always been defined by the people behind it. “Guillaume, Shelley, and Cliff, together with the entire Yangarra team, embody the dedication, craftsmanship, and philosophy that Peter Fraser helped build over so many years. We are proud of this team and confident they will continue to build on Yangarra's remarkable reputation in the years ahead,” Banke said.

The post Yangarra Estate appoints new winemaking and viticulture leadership team appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Guillaume Camougrand, Shelley Torresan and Cliff Wickman. Images supplied

McLaren Vale’s Yangarra Estate has today announced its new winemaking and viticulture leadership team, appointing Guillaume Camougrand as head of winemaking and viticulture, Shelley Torresan as senior winemaker and Cliff Wickman as vineyard manager.

The appointments follow the passing of Peter Fraser, who served as Yangarra’s head winemaker and general manager from 2000 to November 2025.

Yangarra Estate said that Fraser was instrumental in establishing it as “one of Australia’s great wine estates”, championing southern Rhône varieties like Grenache and “defining the philosophy that continues to guide the estate today”.

Chairman and proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, Barbara Banke, said that Yangarra has always been defined by the people behind it.

“Guillaume, Shelley, and Cliff, together with the entire Yangarra team, embody the dedication, craftsmanship, and philosophy that Peter Fraser helped build over so many years. We are proud of this team and confident they will continue to build on Yangarra’s remarkable reputation in the years ahead,” Banke said.

Camougrand joined Yangarra in 2014 and was promoted to assistant winemaker in 2017, working across the full scope of production alongside Fraser and Torresan. In his new role, the winery shared that he is responsible for the estate’s overall winemaking and viticulture direction.

Guillaume Camougrand

Since joining Yangarra in 2008, the winery said that Torresan has been central to the winemaking team.

Shelley Torresan

“Her deep understanding of the wines, meticulous attention to detail, and unwavering focus on quality have helped ensure the consistency and quality of the Yangarra wine portfolio,” the company said.

Wickman joined Yangarra in 2025, bringing more than a decade of vineyard experience, with a particular interest in Rhône varieties and old bush vines. He oversees stewardship of the estate’s 170 hectares, including approximately 87 hectares under vine.

Cliff Wickman

title: From Cabernet to climate-ready – The Granite Belt’s variety shift
summary:
Climate change is affecting many agricultural industries, and winemaking, with its crops always at the mercy of the elements, is one of the most at risk. The Granite Belt, nudging the Queensland-New South Wales border, is one wine region that’s been turning its back on more traditional varieties for a long time. Its embrace of grapes that are more suited to these changing climatic conditions has unlocked new opportunities for growth at a very challenging time. The Granite Belt has faced somewhat of an identity crisis for years: far from major cities like Brisbane and the Gold Coast; believed to be a scorching or subtropical area; and not “serious” when it comes to wine. Yet many people, both consumers and those within the wine industry, have come to discover that there’s quality wine being made in this relatively cool region – much of it from alternative varieties – and it’s worth the travel time.

The post From Cabernet to climate-ready – The Granite Belt’s variety shift appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Brendan Black

Climate change is affecting many agricultural industries, and winemaking, with its crops always at the mercy of the elements, is one of the most at risk. The Granite Belt, nudging the Queensland-New South Wales border, is one wine region that’s been turning its back on more traditional varieties for a long time. Its embrace of grapes that are more suited to these changing climatic conditions has unlocked new opportunities for growth at a very challenging time.

The Granite Belt has faced somewhat of an identity crisis for years: far from major cities like Brisbane and the Gold Coast; believed to be a scorching or subtropical area; and not “serious” when it comes to wine. Yet many people, both consumers and those within the wine industry, have come to discover that there’s quality wine being made in this relatively cool region – much of it from alternative varieties – and it’s worth the travel time.

In fact, the Granite Belt has totally welcomed the non-traditional with ‘Strange Birds’, producing a wine guide that highlights grapes “grown on the Granite Belt [which] represent less than 1% of Australia’s vines” and the 33 wineries that grow them¹. Below are four wineries that have flocked to the Strange Birds.

 

Planting for the future

Mike Hayes is a fourth-generation Granite Belt local and the winemaker at Balancing Heart Vineyard. He’s an extremely knowledgeable and passionate man who’s now entered his 47th year working in the wine industry. In his 40s he undertook a Masters, looking at emerging grape varieties that would be suitable for planting in Australia to withstand climate change.

Hayes researched 650 varieties throughout 50 European wine regions, ultimately setting up a “vineyard of the future” in Queensland with 80 varieties. But there is still a lot of work to be done to convince people of the benefits of these alternate varieties for Australian conditions.

“I did some lectures and was talking about some of the varieties I’d found that I felt they should be looking at,” explained Hayes.

“They still wanted to grow Cabernet, Shiraz, Semillon or Chardonnay and I said, ‘Look, do you realise that the way the climate is altering, you may not be able to grow those varieties?” There are grapes grown on the Iberian Peninsula that can withstand temperatures up to 52°C, but people are still wrapped up in tradition.”

Growing within the Balancing Heart vineyard are some of the usual suspects – Cabernet, Shiraz, Viognier, Chardonnay and Pinot Gris – but also late-ripening varieties such as Mourvèdre, Carmenère, Sagrantino and Refosco del Peduncolo Rosso; there are also 35 rows of Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains that are now over 70 years old. Hayes believes Georgian varieties would also grow well here (there’s already some Saperavi plantedin the region), likewise Greeks such as Assyrtiko and Agiorgitiko, and Cypriots like Maratheftiko and Xynisteri.

A short distance away is Dear Vincent winery, run by Adam Penberthy, who’s also the proprietor of the Ballandean Pub, and was one of the founders of City Winery in Brisbane. He moved to the region looking for a “weekender” but instead bought a vineyard and the pub. Penberthy is “interested in making fun wines that people can drink with mates, rather than with Nana on a Sunday,” although many grandmas seem to love his pet nats.

Most of the vineyard was planted around 25 years ago, with some traditional varieties but also Fiano, Montepulciano, Nebbiolo, Viognier and Verdelho.

“All consumers are starting to embrace alternative varieties,” said Penberthy.

“There are diehards who prefer certain grapes – ‘I only drink Cabernet, I don’t drink Shiraz’ – but most people are intrigued by the alternatives. And the beauty of this region is there’s no history or deep-rooted traditions we need to uphold from a winemaking perspective.”

Despite the embrace of alternative varieties within the region, the subject of climate change leads to much debate.

“If you ask some of the older wine folk in the region, they might say that climate change is really just the normal ups and downs with vintages early, vintages late.Even in my short time here, vintage has gotten earlier and earlier every year, but then if I ask other people, they might say ‘oh, it did that 25 years ago as well’.”

Penberthy’s neighbour to the north is Rokkwell vineyard, purchased by Dawn Walker and partner Jaymie in 2019. Walker then put her Bachelor of Science degree (majoring in Wine Science and Plant Agricultural Science) to good use, becoming assistant winemaker at Ridgemill Estate near Stanthorpe, before moving to full-time at her own winery in 2021.

The vineyard is planted predominantly to Italian varieties: Nebbiolo, Nero d’Avola, Teroldego, Arneis and Garganega, with a small parcel of Sauvignon Blanc. “A lot of people who come to the Granite Belt, they either already know about the ‘Strange Birds’, or they don’t and they learn about them and they find that everywhere they go there might be something different. It’s one of the reasons I was drawn to the Granite Belt myself.”

Walker has also seen climatic changes in the vineyard, with the 2026 vintage being a particularly warm and speedy one.

“Everything grew very quickly,” she detailed.

“I had veraison in mid- December, whereas it would usually be between Christmas and the New Year, so everything was a bit rushed this time, yet we’re definitely still a cool-climate region.”

A few kilometres west of the township of Ballandean is Golden Grove Estate, established in 1946 by Mario and Nita Costanzo as a stone fruit and grape farm. Eventually, the grapes won out and more varieties were planted, first by son Sam, and now the winery is run by grandson Ray and wife Jenny.

Like at Balancing Heart, there are some old rows of Muscat, which was originally grown as a table grape in the area until people realised the liquid gold it could create. The Shiraz vines are 60 years old and the Cabernet is 40, but it’s some of the younger, alternative grapes that are gaining attention. From 2002, other varieties planted include Tempranillo, Barbera, Mourvèdre, Vermentino, Nero d’Avola, Sangiovese and Montepulciano – several of which are medal and trophy winners.

Drought, the last of which ended in 2020, is always around the corner, and with a moratorium on new dams, vines are regularly under pressure.

“The soils drain quickly so you need to keep up the irrigation. Plus, as there are shallow rock shelves, some of the vines, such as Barbera, can still become stressed.”

Since Ray began working at the winery a few decades ago, like winemakers in many other regions, he’s noticed vintages getting shorter.

“We’ve come forward at least a month in picking time,” he said.

“This will be the first Easter that I’ll ever have off as a winemaker.”

 

Planting to site, not fashion

Across the road from Golden Grove is Ballandean Estate, established by Sicilian immigrant Salvatore Cardillo in the 1930s and now the oldest continually running winery in Queensland. In 1968, the table grapes were replaced by Shiraz by Angelo Puglisi, Salvatore’s grandson, who is seen by many as the founder of Queensland’s wine industry. Alongside wife Mary, he later planted Cabernet, Chardonnay, Semillon and Riesling; today, there’s also Silvaner, Fiano, Saperavi, Nebbiolo, Moscato Giallo and Malvasia.

“We used to plant what was popular because it was hard to sell anything else, but now we plant to site – we find out what’s going to suit us best, hence the success of the Saperavi,” said Leeanne Puglisi-Gangemi, Angelo and Mary’s daughter and the client relations manager.

“In the very early days, it was what the customer wanted, but now the weirder it is and as long as it’s grown in the Granite Belt and we call it a Strange Bird, people want to drink it.”

“It’s been an exciting thing to do, and it’s allowed grapegrowers like Dad to make more informed decisions; he can go out there and find these varieties that suit our patch, rather than having to worry about if the customer will actually buy it.”

While there doesn’t seem to be any consensus among the winemakers on the causes of climate change – natural versus anthropogenic – there’s wide agreement that the climate is indeed changing, with both negative and, unexpectedly, positive consequences.

“Some of the traditional varieties are doing better now,” said Puglisi-Gangemi.

“The Cabernet often wouldn’t be ripe until May, yet it’s already picked now [in March]. Dad believes it’s a cycle and more traditional timings will return, but if it is a permanent change, I’m not sure it’s a bad thing.”

Nevertheless, this seems to be one Australian wine region which is preparing itself well against climate change with its embrace of alternative varieties.

As Hayes says, “One good thing about the Granite Belt is that we’ve shown the rest of Australia it can be done.”

content:
Whitehaven’s Sauvignon Blanc has been well-awarded this year. Image supplied

Whitehaven’s Sauvignon Blanc 2025 has been awarded a 93-point rating from Robert Parker Wine Advocate, following a Diamond Trophy at the Sakura Japan Women’s Wine Awards and a Gold Medal at Mundus Vini.

The winery said that the score places the Sauvignon Blanc “firmly within the ‘outstanding’ category” on the publication’s scale.

Wine critic Erin Larkin highlighted its “intensity and precision” in her review, describing it as “piercing and intense” with citrus, green apple and tropical fruit notes. She noted the wine was saline and pure.

“The 2025 vintage showcases everything we love about Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc – vibrant aromatics, purity of fruit and refreshing acidity – and we are delighted to see it recognised by such a respected international authority,” she said.

Whitehaven produces the “#1 Ultra-Premium Sauvignon Blanc in the USA and the #4 Sauvignon Blanc in the USA across all categories”.

content:
The company are finalists in two categories and have been shortlisted for a publicly voted award. Image supplied

Event promoters Ripe Events have been announced as finalists in two categories at the 2026 New Zealand Event Awards.

This year, Ripe Events has been recognised as a finalist for the following categories and corresponding events:

  • Best Food, Beverage or Lifestyle Event: Ripe – The Wānaka Wine & Food Festival
  • Sustainability Initiative of the Year: The Valley Festival

Ripe Events said that the Sustainability Initiative nomination is “particularly meaningful” as the organisation won this same award in 2025 for its work on Ripe Festival, “highlighting the company’s ongoing commitment to creating events that deliver positive environmental and social impact”.

Director Nathan White said that the organisation is incredibly proud to be recognised again by the New Zealand Event Association.

“Winning two awards last year was an amazing achievement for our team, and to return this year with two finalist nominations is a reflection of the passion, innovation and commitment that goes into every event we deliver.

“We’re especially proud to see both ‘Ripe – The Wānaka Wine & Food Festival’ and ‘The Valley Festival’ recognised. These events represent the collaborative relationships we’ve built with our local community in mind, and we’re grateful to everyone who has helped bring them to life.”

In addition to these finalist nominations, Ripe Festival has also been shortlisted for New Zealand’s Favourite Event – a publicly voted award celebrating the events that have captured the hearts of New Zealanders.

Ripe Events is calling on attendees, partners, suppliers and supporters to help Ripe Festival take home the title by casting their vote here or on the company’s social pages.

“To be recognised by industry peers is incredibly rewarding, but to be recognised by the people who attend and support our events would be something truly special. We’d love the community to get behind Ripe Festival and help us bring home New Zealand’s Favourite Event,” said White.

The New Zealand Event Awards celebrate excellence across New Zealand’s events industry, recognising organisations and professionals who demonstrate outstanding creativity, innovation and event delivery.

The winners of the 2026 New Zealand Event Awards will be announced later this year.

title: Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone
summary:
Bowen Estate in Coonawarra is celebrating its 50th vintage this month, an achievement that Coonawarra Vignerons says shows the enduring commitment of a family whose name has become synonymous with classic Coonawarra Cabernet Sauvignon. Family owned and operated since its foundation in 1975 by Doug and Joy Bowen, the winery “has remained true to its philosophy of crafting wines that faithfully express the unique character of Coonawarra's celebrated terra rossa soils”. Reflecting on his journey, Doug Bowen said that he never wanted to work for anyone else. “I had lots of energy and passion to start my own winery experience. I never wanted to work for anyone else, simply wanted to put all my efforts into my own project.” Doug had studied agriculture before completing Oenology at Roseworthy Agricultural College, but it was one unforgettable bottle of wine that cemented his future. “I was fortunate in the late ’60s to share a bottle of Mildara Coonawarra Cabernet 1962. It was a moment in my life I will always remember. My passion and belief in Coonawarra was born there and then.” That conviction led Doug and Joy to establish Bowen Estate, believing the region’s famed terra rossa soils could produce wines of exceptional character and longevity.

The post Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Doug Bowen and his daughter Emma Bowen. Images supplied.

Bowen Estate in Coonawarra is celebrating its 50th vintage this month, an achievement that Coonawarra Vignerons says shows the enduring commitment of a family whose name has become synonymous with classic Coonawarra Cabernet Sauvignon.

Family owned and operated since its foundation in 1975 by Doug and Joy Bowen, the winery “has remained true to its philosophy of crafting wines that faithfully express the unique character of Coonawarra’s celebrated terra rossa soils”.

Reflecting on his journey, Doug Bowen said that he never wanted to work for anyone else.

“I had lots of energy and passion to start my own winery experience. I never wanted to work for anyone else, simply wanted to put all my efforts into my own project.”

Bowen Estate in the 1970’s.

Doug had studied agriculture before completing Oenology at Roseworthy Agricultural College, but it was one unforgettable bottle of wine that cemented his future.

“I was fortunate in the late ’60s to share a bottle of Mildara Coonawarra Cabernet 1962. It was a moment in my life I will always remember. My passion and belief in Coonawarra was born there and then.”

That conviction led Doug and Joy to establish Bowen Estate, believing the region’s famed terra rossa soils could produce wines of exceptional character and longevity.

Doug and Joy believed from the start that great wine begins in the vineyard, with Coonawarra Vignerons highlighting their “hands-on approach, attention to detail and unwavering belief in the quality of their fruit” as defining characteristics of the winery.

“To make the best wine you have to have the best grapes. End of story,” said Doug.

That belief continues to hold up today, where the couple’s daughter Emma Bowen leads the winemaking with support from Doug.

“With Emma in the winery I am immensely proud of what she is doing and has achieved.

“I have stepped back from the winery largely over the past few vintages and she has accepted the new responsibility well. Fortunately, we both share the same philosophy and commitment to our style,” he said.

For Emma, the 50th milestone is as much about honouring the generations before her as it is about looking towards the future.

“Bowen Estate has never just been where I work—it’s where I grew up. Vintage was part of our family life, and some of my fondest memories are of watching my parents in the winery and vineyard.

“To now stand beside Dad as we celebrate and release our 50th vintage is incredibly humbling. It represents everything my parents have built over the past five decades, and I’m honoured to help carry that legacy forward,” she said.

To celebrate the occasion, exclusive trade tastings have been hosted by Emma Bowen in Sydney and Melbourne, introducing the milestone release while sharing the family’s story with leading members of the Australian wine trade.

Moreover, visitors to the Bowen Estate cellar door this month will be among the first to experience the landmark 50th vintage before its broader release.

Coonawarra Vignerons extended congratulations to the Bowen family on the milestone, calling it a “remarkable achievement and a fitting celebration of one of Coonawarra’s great family wine stories”.

title: Aussie wineries celebrate historic wins at Royal Queensland Awards
summary:
Landmark wins at the Royal Queensland Awards (RQA) are being celebrated by wineries around the country. Margaret River based winery Stella Bella saw its Cabernet Sauvignon 2024 claim the Angelo Puglisi Grand Champion Trophy, taking home a $12,000 fellowship for travel, training and education. The wine also won the Stodart Trophy for Best Red Wine and the Alan Cook MBE Trophy for Best Cabernet Sauvignon. Chief judge Julian Langworthy said the wine was a clear standout in the competition. “The Stella Bella Cabernet Sauvignon 2024 had great freshness and real vibrancy of fruit, but a lovely fine tannin structure.” Stella Bella chief winemaker Jarrad Olsen said the recognition meant a great deal to the team. The Dr Bill Ryan Trophy for Best Pinot Noir, which includes a $5,000 bursary, was awarded to Giant Steps for their Sexton Vineyard Pinot Noir 2025, while The Star Trophy for Best Queensland Wine was taken out by Golden Grove Estate for its Nero d’Avola 2025. Adelaide Hills producer Sidewood Estate won Gold for its 2025 Pinot Noir, making it the first Pinot Noir from the region to win Gold since the competition’s records became publicly available in 2015.

The post Aussie wineries celebrate historic wins at Royal Queensland Awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Stella Bella, Sidewood Estate and Giant Steps are among the winners. Images supplied.

Landmark wins at the Royal Queensland Awards (RQA) are being celebrated by wineries around the country.

Margaret River based winery Stella Bella saw its Cabernet Sauvignon 2024 claim the Angelo Puglisi Grand Champion Trophy, taking home a $12,000 fellowship for travel, training and education.

The wine also won the Stodart Trophy for Best Red Wine and the Alan Cook MBE Trophy for Best Cabernet Sauvignon.

Chief judge Julian Langworthy said the wine was a clear standout in the competition.

“The Stella Bella Cabernet Sauvignon 2024 had great freshness and real vibrancy of fruit, but a lovely fine tannin structure.

“It rose strongly as the Grand Champion, with a lot of cohesion through the judging panels on that being the top wine.”

Stella Bella chief winemaker Jarrad Olsen said the recognition meant a great deal to the team.

“It proves that we are working collectively towards a common goal and doing an exceptional job of it – the boost in team morale is huge, and it really reinvigorates everyone.

“The 2024 Stella Bella Cabernet stands out from its peers as it is made from 100% estate grown fruit from the south of Margaret River.

“There aren’t many vineyards planted in these cooler areas and, in warmer vintages like 2024, the retention of detail, perfume and energy in these southern Margaret River sites creates a point of difference that really stands out.”

The Dr Bill Ryan Trophy for Best Pinot Noir, which includes a $5,000 bursary, was awarded to Giant Steps for their Sexton Vineyard Pinot Noir 2025, while The Star Trophy for Best Queensland Wine was taken out by Golden Grove Estate for its Nero d’Avola 2025.

Adelaide Hills producer Sidewood Estate won Gold for its 2025 Pinot Noir, making it the first Pinot Noir from the region to win Gold since the competition’s records became publicly available in 2015.

It achieved a score of 95 points, “finishing directly behind the trophy winner in its class”.

Moreover, the Sidewood Owen’s 25 Chardonnay was also awarded Gold with 95 points.

Organisers said this year’s competition attracted 1,164 wines from 178 Australian wineries, judged over four days last week by 25 industry experts.

Langworthy said the overall quality of entries was extremely high, with 128 gold medals awarded.

“Chardonnay was a highlight as usual, with one judge noting the 2025 vintage was the best batch of Chardonnays they had ever judged, and I also really enjoyed the diversity of the Rieslings, as well as the Semillon.

“The Gold medals we awarded across a diverse range of styles are wines people should seek out and enjoy – they show how strong Australian wine is at the moment, with some great gems among them.”

title: First Ridge Wines recruits Clarity Cru Wine Merchants for NSW and ACT distribution
summary:
Mudgee's Italian varietal winegrower First Ridge Wines enlisted Clarity Cru Wine Merchants as its NSW and ACT distributor. Clarity Cru Wine Merchants, based in Lane Cove West, operates as a specialist NSW and ACT distributor with a focus on “quality producers and a trade-first approach”. The businesses said their unique stories and shared attention to detail make them well aligned. "We have built something genuinely distinctive at First Ridge. Italian varieties, grown on our own estate, in one of Australia's historic wine regions. The time is right to share that more broadly, and Clarity Cru's commitment to producer relationships and trade service made them the right partner for us," said John Nicholas, owner and vigneron at First Ridge Wines.

content:
First Ridge Wines has entered a distribution partnership with Clarity Cru Wine Merchants. Image supplied

Mudgee’s Italian varietal winegrower First Ridge Wines enlisted Clarity Cru Wine Merchants as its NSW and ACT distributor.

First Ridge Wines, established on ancient rocky ridges south of Mudgee in 1998 “under the guidance of world-renowned viticulturist Dr Richard Smart”, specialises in Italian varieties grown at elevations above 500 metres in the NSW Central Ranges.

Clarity Cru Wine Merchants, based in Lane Cove West, operates as a specialist NSW and ACT distributor with a focus on “quality producers and a trade-first approach”.

The businesses said their unique stories and shared attention to detail make them well aligned.

“We have built something genuinely distinctive at First Ridge. Italian varieties, grown on our own estate, in one of Australia’s historic wine regions. The time is right to share that more broadly, and Clarity Cru’s commitment to producer relationships and trade service made them the right partner for us,” said John Nicholas, owner and vigneron at First Ridge Wines.

Daniel Radford, owner and director at Clarity Cru Wine Merchants, said that First Ridge is exactly the kind of producer they enjoy representing.

“Estate-grown Italian varieties from Mudgee, with genuine quality credentials and a compelling story. These are wines the trade and their customers will engage with. We are delighted to partner with the First Ridge team,” he said.

The estate’s portfolio spans Barbera, Sangiovese, Montepulciano, Fiano, Vermentino, Pinot Grigio, Sangiovese Rosé and Prosecco – wines that have earned the winery a number of recent wine show trophies and inclusion in The Real Review’s 2026 Top Wineries Australia list.

The First Ridge range is available to NSW and ACT trade accounts through Clarity Cru Wine Merchants from July 2026. Direct cellar door sales and the Prima Cresta Wine Club continue to operate through firstridge.com.au.

title: NSW invests $2 million in Hilltops Vineyard for dealcoholisation facility
summary:
The NSW government has invested $2 million for Freeman Vineyards in Young to establish what it claims is "the state’s first dealcoholisation facility". The facility aims to position the Hilltops region as a national leader in the rapidly growing non-alcoholic wine market. Freeman Vineyards was one of nine recipients of funding through the Agriculture Industries Innovation and Growth Program, with owner and founder Dr Brian Freeman and his daughter Marcelle Freeman saying the project will be a game changer for the industry in NSW. “We are really excited. You can make much better-quality wine if you don’t have to transport it far, so we can make much better-quality non-alcoholic wine if we don’t have to transport it far,” Marcelle said. “We’re also putting in a bottling unit, so that can help local producers as well, if they want. We will definitely need to hire more people locally once the facility is up and running.”

The post NSW invests $2 million in Hilltops Vineyard for dealcoholisation facility appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR: Dr Brian Freeman, Steph Cooke MP and Marcelle Freeman. Image supplied.

The NSW government has invested $2 million for Freeman Vineyards in Young to establish what it claims is “the state’s first dealcoholisation facility”.

The facility aims to position the Hilltops region as a national leader in the rapidly growing non-alcoholic wine market.

Freeman Vineyards was one of nine recipients of funding through the Agriculture Industries Innovation and Growth Program, with owner and founder Dr Brian Freeman and his daughter Marcelle Freeman saying the project will be a game changer for the industry in NSW.

“We are really excited. You can make much better-quality wine if you don’t have to transport it far, so we can make much better-quality non-alcoholic wine if we don’t have to transport it far,” Marcelle said.

“We’re also putting in a bottling unit, so that can help local producers as well, if they want.

“We will definitely need to hire more people locally once the facility is up and running.”

The dealcoholisation project and bottling unit means producers no longer need to send their wine interstate for alcohol removal – they can have it made, dealcoholised and bottled all in one place.

Dr Freeman said the facility will help meet growing demand as more consumers, particularly younger people, move towards low and no alcohol options.

“It’s said to be the young generation abstaining from alcohol totally, so there’s that aspect coming into it, as well as the older generation are drinking a lot less.

“But it’s the Hilltops region that has a big advantage, and we’re producing really top-quality grapes, so we can produce a very good quality product on our range of wines that we currently have, but we’ll be able to produce a really good non-alcoholic wine as well,” he said.

Member for Cootamundra Steph Cooke MP also welcomed the investment.

“This is a landmark investment for Freeman Vineyard and the Hilltops wine region, cementing our reputation as a centre for agricultural innovation and premium food and wine production.

“Freeman Vineyards has built an outstanding reputation for producing worldclass wines and this funding will allow them to lead the way in one of the fastest growing segments of the global wine industry,” she said.

Cooke added that the funding is a vote of confidence in the region’s wine industry.

“Establishing New South Wales’s first dealcoholisation facility right here in Young means more value will be added locally, more jobs can be created locally, and more opportunities will flow to our grape growers and winemakers.”

With demand for premium low and no-alcohol wines continuing to grow, Cooke believes the project is a “significant investment into the future of the NSW wine industry”.

“Keeping that capability in regional NSW will reduce transport costs, improve efficiency and strengthen our local manufacturing sector.

“Consumer preferences are evolving and our local producers are evolving with them.

“This funding will deliver long-term benefits not only for Young, but for wineries across the state looking to access this technology closer to home,” she said.

title: New Zealand Winegrowers adds five wine “champions” to roll of Fellows
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) is celebrating its annual honours list by announcing five additions to the roll of NZW Fellows. Fabian Yukich, chair of NZW, says the Fellows are a peer-nominated group of “highly respected and influential individuals” whose leadership, innovation, hard work and passion have helped shape the success of the New Zealand wine industry. “Each year, the Fellows honour recognises a select group of people who have made an outstanding contribution to our industry. The 2026 Fellows have each helped strengthen New Zealand wine in their own way, through their commitment to excellence, generosity of knowledge, and service to the wider wine community,” said Yukich. The 2026 New Zealand Winegrowers Fellows are as follows: Kevin Judd, Lynnette Hudson, Rex Sunde, Philip Gregan and Sam Neill.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers adds five wine “champions” to roll of Fellows appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
LTR, Top to bottom: Lynette Hudson, Kevin Judd, Philip Gregan, Sam Neill and Rex Sunde. Images supplied

New Zealand Winegrowers (NZW) is celebrating its annual honours list by announcing five additions to the roll of NZW Fellows.

Fabian Yukich, chair of NZW, says the Fellows are a peer-nominated group of “highly respected and influential individuals” whose leadership, innovation, hard work and passion have helped shape the success of the New Zealand wine industry.

“Each year, the Fellows honour recognises a select group of people who have made an outstanding contribution to our industry. The 2026 Fellows have each helped strengthen New Zealand wine in their own way, through their commitment to excellence, generosity of knowledge, and service to the wider wine community,” said Yukich.

The 2026 New Zealand Winegrowers Fellows are as follows:

  • Kevin Judd – for promoting New Zealand wine and photography

Judd is recognised for his contribution to New Zealand wine’s global reputation, from his role as founding winemaker at Cloudy Bay to establishing Greywacke, and for his distinctive photography capturing the vineyards, regions and people of the industry.

  • Lynnette Hudson – for services to wine education and Pinot Noir

Hudson’s winemaking expertise and passion for Pinot Noir has led to a longstanding contribution to wine education and industry leadership. She has generously shared her knowledge through the Southern Pinot Noir Workshop, which she has been on the committee and deeply involved with for over 25 years.

  • Rex Sunde – for services to viticulture and sustainability

Sunde has contributed immensely to New Zealand Winegrowers’ Vineyard Spray Schedule, and his service has included time as a New Zealand Winegrowers board member, a member of the New Zealand Winegrowers research committee and an executive committee member for Organic Winegrowers New Zealand.

  • Philip Gregan – for extensive service to the industry and to NZW

Gregan’s outstanding contribution to the New Zealand wine industry has been through over four decades of leadership and service. His tenure saw him play a pivotal role in international trade relations, along with steering the industry to an export-focused global success story. After 43 years of service to the organisation, including 35 years as CEO across the Wine Institute and New Zealand Winegrowers, Gregan retired at the end of June 2026.

  • Sam Neill – recognised for promoting New Zealand wine.

As an internationally acclaimed actor and tireless advocate for the industry over many years, Sam has generously used his profile, talents and distinctive voice to promote New Zealand wine, bringing authenticity, warmth and global attention to the people, places and stories behind the industry.

“We are incredibly proud of this year’s Fellows and the contributions they have made,” said Yukich.

“They leave a lasting legacy for the next generation of winegrowers, and I look forward to acknowledging and congratulating them at their induction at the end of August.”

The 2026 Fellows will be inducted at the New Zealand Wine 2026 Celebration Dinner, being held in Pōneke Wellington at the end of August.

title: Paolo Saccone is Australia’s Sommelier of the Year
summary:

content:
Winner Paolo Saccone MS presenting at his exam. Images credit Isabella Wild

Sommeliers Australia has announced Paolo Saccone MS (LUCAS Restaurants, NSW) as the official winner of the ‘2026 Sommelier Challenge: Finding Australia’s Best Sommelier of the Year’.

The title was unveiled Monday night at a sold-out black-tie dinner ceremony hosted at the Lee Ho Fook in Sydney following an “intensive day of national finals”.

“I’m excited and honoured to be named Australia’s Best Sommelier at this year’s Sommelier Challenge. The calibre of this year’s group was incredibly high – there was no room for error in the competition, and it challenged us to be the best we could possibly be. I’m grateful to be included in such a talented group of candidates,” Saccone said.

As the newly crowned winner, Saccone will represent Australia on the global stage, travelling travel to Lisbon in Portugal from 11–17 October 2026 to compete on behalf of Sommeliers Australia at the ‘Association de la Sommellerie Internationale (ASI) Best Sommelier of the World 2026’ competition.

“I’m ready for the task ahead in Portugal. I have a couple of months to train my tasting skills, theory and practical elements to prepare myself for any scenario. It would be a dream come true to come out on top at the Best Sommelier of the World competition and shine a light on the quality of the sommelier industry in Australia,” he said.

Sommeliers Australia said Saccone triumphed over an “exceptionally competitive field of the nation’s top wine professionals”, which included fellow finalists Isaac Pockney (Bennelong, NSW); Clotilde Candusso (Freyja, VIC); Justin Biskup (CARPE VINO, VIC); and wild card finalist Charlie Richards (Agnes, QLD).

The final stage of the competition took place early Monday in the Sydney Tower at the Infinity by Mark Best restaurant. The five finalists faced a “gruelling gauntlet” of written, tasting, practical and theory examinations “designed to test technical knowledge and industry expertise at the absolute highest standards – reflecting Master Sommelier, WSET Diploma, and ASI Diploma levels”.

“The calibre of talent displayed at this year’s Sommelier Challenge has been nothing short of world-class, and we are incredibly proud to crown Saccone as Australia’s Best Sommelier for 2026,” said Sommeliers Australia president Louella Mathews.

“This 2026 Sommelier Challenge is the modern reboot of Australia’s Best Sommelier of the Year. Beyond being a competition, it provides a pathway for professionals to demonstrate their knowledge on the world stage and engage with the global sommelier community. It strengthens Australia’s wine and hospitality sector by elevating standards of service, deepening collective knowledge and fostering the exchange of expertise across peers and the next generation of sommeliers.

“Saccone demonstrated extraordinary technical precision, theoretical depth, and flawless hospitality under immense pressure, and we are confident he will represent our nation with immense pride and distinction in Lisbon this October.”

Mathews also extended her gratitude to the competition’s partners.

“The Sommelier Challenge would not be possible without the generous support of our Gold Partners: Ruinart, House of Arras, Jim Barry and Pewsey Vale.

“Their commitment to excellence in wine service and education helps elevate the profession across Australia. This collaboration reflects a shared dedication to strengthening industry standards, enhancing brand engagement, and fostering meaningful connections with key industry decision-makers throughout the wine and hospitality sectors.”

title: Wine Growth Summit facilitates sharing of international expertise with Aussie industry professionals
summary:
The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia. Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years. Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand. Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries. Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, shared lessons from the Poco Vino brand story. Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them and chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

content:
By Kyra Trussell

The 2026 Wine Growth Summit hosted by Wine Communicators of Australia was recently held at the Adelaide Convention Centre, with over 15 guest speakers bringing international wine industry insights and strategies to Australia.

Speaker Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia, highlighted that Brazil, Mexico, Thailand and India are all expected to import more wine over the next five years.

Angelica Crabb, senior analyst at Wine Australia

“This increase won’t offset the larger decreases in the bigger markets on a global scale, but for individual businesses willing to diversify, these could be markets worth taking a closer look at,” she said.

Crabb also drew attention to Japan being “second only in white wine consumption to Australia in the Asia Pacific” before detailing other key export countries in the region.

“Looking at Australian exports of white wines to key Asian destinations, you can see that there’s a generally positive trend to destinations like Hong Kong, Thailand, Singapore and South Korea, but what really stands out are the exports to China – they are significantly different now than before the tariffs. 17% of the volume of exports to China is now white wine, and it was 4% in 2020.”

Crabb reassured the audience that it’s not all sour grapes for red wine exports.

“If you exclude China and Hong Kong, Cab Sav exports are up by 30% in value in the last 12 months. Cabernet Sauvignon is growing very well into Canada at the moment, but the next four markets that are growing are Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Indonesia. Basically, the Southeast Asian markets are drinking more premium Cabernet Sauvignon from Australia.

“Red is a key wine style in these markets, and it’s a very similar story to Shiraz. For Shiraz, excluding China and Hong Kong, the growth is 7% year on year. The fastest growing market is Thailand, followed by Singapore, Canada and Malaysia,” she shared.

Brendan Carter, winemaker at Unico Zello and director of media company Bottle Shock, spoke of the dynamic and youthful wine culture in Thailand.

Brendan Carter, Unico Zello winemaker and director of Bottle Shock

“The thing that got me was the culture between different venues…the operators help each other; it’s a part of their culture. The attitude is very much ‘if someone gets excited about wine, they’re going to go into my neighbour’s venue, and that is a net good for everyone’. They even host each other’s events to try to act as activations, so they’re very, very close,” he revealed.

Carter also detailed the lack of a gender equality issue in Thailand’s wine industry.

“I went to about 25 wine bars in a week, across all of them, all of them, the importers, the buyers, owners, sommeliers and the people with actual purchasing power, there was simply no gender problem to speak of. Women aren’t really fighting for a seat at the Thai wine table, because they literally built the table.

“Interestingly, when I questioned this, they thought it was the dumbest question I could ask. It’s not framed as an achievement in Thailand, because it’s just not an issue, which is his own kind of remark.”

As Carter frankly put it, Thailand is “just f*cking around and finding out”.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7, offered insights from the e-commerce platforms’ top performing wineries.

Andrew Kamphuis, founder and president of Commerce7

“The best wineries in the world, they capture data. The best wineries capture 92% of email addresses at the cellar door…when we look at the average, it’s about 50% (46%).

“Our top 10% of wineries are emailing their entire customer base an average of 10 times a year. The average winery only sends two and a half emails a year. If you’re going to build lifetime customers, if you’re going to attract people, you need to capture an email, and you need to send it,” he said.

Kamphuis also emphasised the importance of personalising the experience online for customers, suggesting winery websites should recommend products on the home page based on previous purchases and information regarding flavour preferences collected at the cellar door. Including a simple ‘welcome back’ message with the customer’s name when they log in can also be a nice touch.

There were many domestic success stories told, with Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage, sharing lessons from the Poco Vino brand story.

Emily Harper, chief marketing officer at Australian Vintage

Harper said that the first thing the winery did before planning a new product at all was research. The company spoke to over 3000 people globally to find out what challenges people were facing with wine, what the barriers are, tensions and what the brand is “solving for”.

She stressed the importance of looking outside of wine for inspiration, with the packing and retail display designs for Poco Vino being reportedly influenced by the beauty and cosmetic category, coffee brands and the trend of mini chocolates.

The team behind the little wine also thought about the moments where customers would typically reach for RTDs, spirits and seltzers, asking themselves how wine could play a role in those spaces instead.

“We’ve been in market for eight months, we are now ranged in 17 countries and we’re selling something in the region of 500 bottles every hour of this product – it’s genuinely blown our minds,” Harper shared.

“…but the story of this brand is not the vineyard and the terroir and the provenance – it’s actually just how it fits in your lifestyle and that it’s easy, it’s delicious and it’s accessible…even the tasting notes literally just have these three words on the back, there’s no spiel, we stripped everything back and pulled it back to its kind of most raw but fun state.

“We’re not selling a Shiraz…we’re selling the moment that you’re drinking it. We’re selling joy, we’re selling connection, we’re selling that moment where you’re sitting in that meal…that’s what we’re selling. So, bring that to people, and bring that to people in a really engaging way,” she advised.

Bringing an Adelaide local lens and down-to-Earth spirit to the event, Louis Schofield of World Apart Wines encouraged the audience to rethink what growth means to them.

Louis Schofield, founder of World Apart Wines

“Today is about growth, and there’s this great paradox of the industry is not growing, but we keep talking about how we grow. For me, growing is not necessarily about making more money, making more wine, you know, getting more employees, whatever it is…but we do still want to diversify and have some different markets.

“We’ve recently got rid of all of our distributors in Australia, and we now sell the wine ourselves in every market, so I’m doing a lot of domestic travel. I think there’s a great question that keeps coming back to me – why do we need to keep growing?

“These are the things that are important to us in our winery. It isn’t trying to get to 10,000 cases, it isn’t trying to make a million dollars a year turnover – it’s being in the winery with our little kids, it’s being resilient, it’s being sustainable and it’s spending time in my garden. That’s what we love. So, that’s what the growth means to us,” he affirmed.

On the topic of expanding into new markets, chief operating officer for the Randall Wine Group Alexia Roberts stressed the importance of showing up in person and having patience.

Alexia Roberts, chief operating officer at Randall Wine Group

“You know, I think COVID taught us all that we could do stuff by teams, and we all thought, ‘beauty, days of getting on planes are over’, but the reality is that nothing is better than having a conversation in person, face-to-face. Showing up for success is absolutely critical.

“This is an incredibly complex market – some people find it too hard, to be honest, because there are so many nuances in this market – but I promise you, if you take the time and effort to understand it by province, not by country, you will be rewarded,” she said.

Roberts also shared how being flexible can lead to unexpected success, using the launch of the One Pound Per Acre brand as an example.

“Our strategy was about growing premium brands globally, and all of a sudden, we were looking at a $9.95 proposition on the shelf for Chardonnay and Shiraz – but guess what, China was closed, and it seemed like a good opportunity at the time. So, we pivoted. There was an opportunity in front of us, and we pivoted, and we built this brand.

“We launched it in September 2023 with two wines, Chardonnay and Shiraz, and we launched it to fill a gap in the Australian category in Ontario. So [in] one province we launched this wine. 18 months from launch date we were the sixth top Australian wine brand by sales in the LCDO.”

title: Yangarra Estate appoints new winemaking and viticulture leadership team
summary:
McLaren Vale’s Yangarra Estate has today announced its new winemaking and viticulture leadership team, appointing Guillaume Camougrand as head of winemaking and viticulture, Shelley Torresan as senior winemaker and Cliff Wickman as vineyard manager. The appointments follow the passing of Peter Fraser, who served as Yangarra's head winemaker and general manager from 2000 to November 2025. Chairman and proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, Barbara Banke, said that Yangarra has always been defined by the people behind it. “Guillaume, Shelley, and Cliff, together with the entire Yangarra team, embody the dedication, craftsmanship, and philosophy that Peter Fraser helped build over so many years. We are proud of this team and confident they will continue to build on Yangarra's remarkable reputation in the years ahead,” Banke said.

The post Yangarra Estate appoints new winemaking and viticulture leadership team appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Guillaume Camougrand, Shelley Torresan and Cliff Wickman. Images supplied

McLaren Vale’s Yangarra Estate has today announced its new winemaking and viticulture leadership team, appointing Guillaume Camougrand as head of winemaking and viticulture, Shelley Torresan as senior winemaker and Cliff Wickman as vineyard manager.

The appointments follow the passing of Peter Fraser, who served as Yangarra’s head winemaker and general manager from 2000 to November 2025.

Yangarra Estate said that Fraser was instrumental in establishing it as “one of Australia’s great wine estates”, championing southern Rhône varieties like Grenache and “defining the philosophy that continues to guide the estate today”.

Chairman and proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, Barbara Banke, said that Yangarra has always been defined by the people behind it.

“Guillaume, Shelley, and Cliff, together with the entire Yangarra team, embody the dedication, craftsmanship, and philosophy that Peter Fraser helped build over so many years. We are proud of this team and confident they will continue to build on Yangarra’s remarkable reputation in the years ahead,” Banke said.

Camougrand joined Yangarra in 2014 and was promoted to assistant winemaker in 2017, working across the full scope of production alongside Fraser and Torresan. In his new role, the winery shared that he is responsible for the estate’s overall winemaking and viticulture direction.

Guillaume Camougrand

Since joining Yangarra in 2008, the winery said that Torresan has been central to the winemaking team.

Shelley Torresan

“Her deep understanding of the wines, meticulous attention to detail, and unwavering focus on quality have helped ensure the consistency and quality of the Yangarra wine portfolio,” the company said.

Wickman joined Yangarra in 2025, bringing more than a decade of vineyard experience, with a particular interest in Rhône varieties and old bush vines. He oversees stewardship of the estate’s 170 hectares, including approximately 87 hectares under vine.

Cliff Wickman

title: From Cabernet to climate-ready – The Granite Belt’s variety shift
summary:
Climate change is affecting many agricultural industries, and winemaking, with its crops always at the mercy of the elements, is one of the most at risk. The Granite Belt, nudging the Queensland-New South Wales border, is one wine region that’s been turning its back on more traditional varieties for a long time. Its embrace of grapes that are more suited to these changing climatic conditions has unlocked new opportunities for growth at a very challenging time. The Granite Belt has faced somewhat of an identity crisis for years: far from major cities like Brisbane and the Gold Coast; believed to be a scorching or subtropical area; and not “serious” when it comes to wine. Yet many people, both consumers and those within the wine industry, have come to discover that there’s quality wine being made in this relatively cool region – much of it from alternative varieties – and it’s worth the travel time.
